segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with ethereal, high-pitched synth sounds over a dark, starry background, quickly transitioning to sweeping aerial views of the snow-capped Rocky Mountains under a clear blue sky, showcasing their rugged terrain and vastness. A deep, authoritative male voiceover begins, accompanied by dramatic orchestral music with percussion, declaring, "The Rocky Mountains. The backbone of North America," and describing them as "rugged, spectacular, and majestic." The camera glides over jagged peaks, glaciers, and valleys, before briefly cutting to an older scientist in a lab, examining a sample under a microscope, as the voiceover explains that "Today geologists are cracking open these rocks to expose their secrets." The scene returns to the mountains with dynamic cloud movements and changing light, while the voiceover poses intriguing questions: "How did the remains of sea creatures end up in mountain peaks, thousands of feet in the air?" The view then zooms out to Earth from space, highlighting North America, as the voiceover continues, "When did the Rockies form? Are they still rising today?" A cosmic setting with floating asteroids and scientific equations overlays the visuals, and the voiceover speculates, "And if they are, could the great forces that built the Rockies one day split North America in two?" The music shifts to a driving, action-oriented orchestral piece with strong percussion and brass as a title card for 'NAKED SCIENCE: THE ROCKIES' appears with the National Geographic logo. The segment concludes with a panoramic view of the snow-covered Rockies under a partly cloudy sky, reinforcing the theme of geological and natural wonder, with the intense orchestral music continuing.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with sweeping aerial views of The Rockies, showcasing their jagged, snow-covered peaks under a bright blue sky with drifting clouds, as a male narrator introduces them as "one of the greatest mountain chains on Earth." Throughout, a dramatic, suspenseful orchestral score underscores the visuals. The narrator details their immense scale, stretching "over 2000 miles from the northern tip of British Columbia to the Rio Grande in New Mexico," and being "up to 300 miles wide," forming "the continental divide, the great barrier between East and West." The visuals continue to display the majestic Rockies as the narrator highlights that "in Colorado alone, there are 58 mountain peaks over 14,000 feet tall," clarifying this is "around two and a half miles high." The scene then transitions to a rotating Earth from Space, highlighting North and South America, followed by close-ups of reddish-brown rock formations, as the narrator explains that "The Rockies were created by the most powerful geologic forces on Earth." The dramatic music swells. The sequence shifts to a dynamic underwater view of a rocky seabed with a turquoise hue, then to a wide valley with green forests and winding rivers, and finally to a dramatic, mist-shrouded mountain range with a calm lake reflecting the overcast sky. During these transitions, the narrator continues, stating that "Their dramatic story takes us on a journey from north to south through 200 million years of geological history," before the audio cuts off.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a serene, wide-angle shot of a calm lake reflecting snow-capped mountains, identified by a male narrator as 'this place in Canada,' before he mentions 'the youngest peaks in the United States.' This tranquil natural setting, featuring a mist-covered mountain range and dense evergreen forests, transitions into a dynamic time-lapse where the lighting shifts dramatically from cool blues to warm golden hues, suggesting the passage of time from dusk to dawn, with clouds moving swiftly across the sky. Upbeat, slightly dramatic electronic music begins as the narrator explains this is 'a journey that takes us back into the Earth's ancient past.' The scene then shifts to a rotating view of Earth from space, revealing North and South America against a starry backdrop. The narrator states that '200 million years ago, there were no Rockies,' and 'even the continents looked different,' explaining there was 'just one giant landmass, Pangea.' The camera zooms in on the Mid-Atlantic Ridge with a detailed topographical view, as the narrator warns, 'but not for much longer.' The Earth rotates further, and a red line appears, tracing the boundaries of tectonic plates, emphasizing the separation of continents. A deep, rumbling tearing sound accompanies the narrator's description of 'a vast rift is opening up, tearing this supercontinent apart.' The continents are then shown in a fiery, molten state, illustrating continental drift and plate tectonics, as the narrator continues, 'Pangea will break apart into many smaller landmasses,' eventually 'forming the present-day continent of North America.' A deep, resonant clang and hum are heard before the narrator concludes that 'Earth's surface is broken up into vast pieces called plates.'

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dramatic depiction of Earth from space, showing a fiery, molten representation of the continents, emphasizing tectonic activity and continental drift with a red line tracing plate boundaries, as a deep-voiced male narrator explains the "warm, dense mantle" and how "convection currents" slowly move these plates across the Earth's surface. This transitions into a close-up of the Sun’s turbulent surface, glowing with intense orange and red hues, while the narrator continues, detailing how "immense tectonic forces" at plate edges can "tear continents apart" but also "build mountains," accompanied by deep, rumbling and thrumming sounds. The scene then shifts to an underwater seabed, where a dark, elongated object moves through murky, turquoise water, hinting at deep-sea exploration or tectonic activity beneath the ocean. A distinct, almost metallic clang is heard before the narrative cuts to a man, identified by a lower-third graphic as Dr. Kirk R. Johnson from the Denver Museum of Nature & Science, standing outdoors in a rocky, arid landscape with layered red rock formations and sparse vegetation. The narrator introduces him as "Kirk Johnson, chief curator of the Denver Museum of Nature and Science," who "explains." The NAKED SCIENCE graphic appears. Dr. Kirk R. Johnson, speaking directly to the camera with a clear, articulate voice, explains that "the action of continents crushing together or continents being hit by oceanic plates" creates "basically planetary traffic accidents" where "the wrecks result in mountains forming." Dramatic, orchestral background music begins as the video transitions to sweeping aerial views of The Rockies, showcasing snow-capped peaks, dense evergreen forests, and a vibrant turquoise lake nestled in the valley, with dynamic cloud movements and shifting light. The narrator returns, stating that "evidence of the power of these immense forces can be found in one of the oldest and most northerly parts of the Rockies," specifically "Yoho National Park in British Columbia." The final shots focus on rugged, rocky terrain with visible sedimentary layers, emphasizing geological history and erosion, under a partly cloudy sky.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with dramatic, slightly mysterious background music accompanying close-up, black-and-white images of fossilized ancient marine life, showcasing intricate segmented bodies and appendages. A narrator explains these are thousands of peculiar prehistoric creatures found in a cliff face near Mount Stephen, 8,000 feet above sea level. The scene transitions to a rugged mountainous terrain where researchers, including geologist and mountain guide Paul McNeil from the Burgess Shale Geoscience Foundation, are actively collecting rock samples. Paul, wearing a white bucket hat and dark jacket, holds a fossilized specimen and speaks directly to the camera with a clear American accent, stating that these fossils are found almost nowhere else and are unlike any animal alive today, with the NAKED SCIENCE logo visible. The video returns to detailed fossil close-ups, with the narrator identifying a "spiny creature" as Hallucigenia and a "bizarre specimen" as Wiwaxia, emphasizing their preserved soft tissues and anatomical features. A vibrant 3D animated reconstruction then depicts an ancient underwater environment, showing various prehistoric creatures, confirmed by the narrator as sea creatures, moving among colorful tube-like organisms and sponges on a sandy seabed with rising bubbles. The narrator explains these fossils offer clues to how the early Northern Rockies formed, as they are the remains of sea creatures that lived and died in a shallow tropical sea over half a billion years ago, with the rocks containing them formed on the sea floor. The segment concludes with sweeping aerial shots of snow-capped mountain peaks, highlighting the dramatic geological landscape.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a sweeping view of a rugged, snow-dusted mountain peak under a bright blue sky, framed by evergreen trees, establishing a dramatic natural setting. Paul McNeil, identified as being from the 'Burgess Shale Geoscience Foundation', appears in a close-up, wearing a white bucket hat and dark jacket against layered rock formations. He speaks with a clear, informative voice, explaining that the surrounding rocks were once an ancient seabed, with limestones forming part of the continental shelf and shales from deeper ocean waters, all originally submerged. As orchestral background music begins, the camera pans across a dry, rocky cliff face with distinct horizontal strata, emphasizing geological layering. This transitions to an underwater sequence, showing a sunlit seabed with clear turquoise water moving into darker, deeper ocean depths with rocky terrain. A narrator's authoritative voice explains that long before the Rockies formed, the West Coast of North America was covered by a shallow sea where, over millions of years, clay, mud, and gravel settled on the coastal shelf, trapping and fossilizing tens of thousands of sea creatures. The video returns to the terrestrial rock face, now overlaid with a 3D animated reconstruction of colorful prehistoric marine life, including purple spiky creatures and tube-like organisms, moving across a sandy seabed, visually representing the fossilized creatures. A translucent blue overlay appears, indicating a time span of '8000 YEARS' between two horizontal lines on the rock layers, as the narrator specifies that these rock layers were made from sediment laid down over 8,000 years, solidifying into rock up to 12 miles thick. The segment concludes with detailed close-ups of the layered rock, highlighting textures and mineral variations, reinforcing the theme of deep time and fossil preservation, with the narrator adding that these fossils are now a mile and a half above sea level but were once buried six miles under other layers of rock.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of Paul McNeil, wearing a white bucket hat with a dark brim and a dark jacket, speaking directly to the camera in a clear, explanatory male voice. He gestures slightly as he explains how fossils in the layered rock formations behind him were buried over 10 kilometers deep under the Earth's crust and then thrust up over 2 kilometers. A dramatic, resonant sound effect introduces a camera pan across the detailed, stratified rock face, highlighting its horizontal layers in shades of brown, gray, and orange, with visible erosion and mineral variation. The view then expands to a wider landscape showing a rugged, rocky slope with distant snow-capped peaks under a cloudy sky, emphasizing the mountainous terrain. A narrator, with a calm, authoritative male voice, begins to speak, explaining that this ancient rock formed offshore and was pushed inland and upward by a powerful force called "subduction," as subtle, suspenseful background music starts. The scene transitions to a 3D animated Earth from space, rotating to show North and South America, with a red line tracing tectonic plate boundaries. Animated arrows appear, illustrating continental drift and plate movement, as the narrator details how 180 million years ago, the North American plate was moving west and the Pacific Ocean plate was heading east, leading to a "head-on" collision. The animation then shifts to a cross-sectional view of the Earth’s crust, showing two tectonic plates moving apart over a molten mantle, visually explaining the process of seafloor spreading and continental separation, with the narrator describing the denser Pacific Ocean plate being forced down in this "clash of the Titans," all underscored by building, dramatic music and sound effects.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a 3D animated cross-section of Earth's crust, depicting tectonic plates moving over a molten core with arrows indicating subduction, as a male narrator explains how one plate moves "beneath the more buoyant North American plate," accompanied by dramatic, suspenseful music. The scene quickly transitions through a blurred, fast-moving interior shot before settling on Prof. John W. Geissman, identified by a lower-third graphic from 'NAKED SCIENCE', standing in a well-lit office. He begins a demonstration, placing a standard silver CD into a shallow white container filled with a dark, viscous liquid. Speaking with a clear, academic, and enthusiastic tone, he explains, "What I'm going to do is simply tap on one of the sides and I'm going to create an instability." He gently presses the CD, which floats and slowly sinks. As the camera zooms on the submerging CD, then cuts to a close-up of Prof. Geissman, he continues, "Now we have oil starting to migrate, move its way over the top of the CD. Ah! Voila, it's happening all by itself. I don't have to nudge it anymore." He describes the "oil moving its way across the CD" and the CD itself beginning to move "into the oil," completing "the subduction process." Returning to the partially submerged CD, he emphasizes, "It's subducted into the interior of our planet, if you will. And it's a runaway process. You can't stop it." The segment concludes with a return to the animated tectonic plate movement, mirroring the opening, as the dramatic music swells again.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a dramatic, slightly ominous background score, punctuated by rumbling and scraping sound effects, as a 3D animated cross-section of Earth's crust illustrates tectonic plate boundaries. Yellow arrows indicate two continental plates moving horizontally over a molten core. A male narrator explains that offshore islands on the subducting Pacific plate were pushed eastward like a conveyor belt, eventually reaching and slamming into the continental shelf on the western edge of North America. As the plates diverge, a body of water recedes, revealing a widening rift that eventually fills with sediment, forming a new landmass. The narrator details how the seabed was shoved eastward, slowly folding up like a crumpled carpet, pushing the fossils of the Burgess Shale thousands of feet into the air and miles inland. The animation then transitions to a real-world view of a rugged mountain landscape with snow-capped peaks and dense forests under a partly cloudy sky, which the narrator identifies as the Rockies in British Columbia. The scene cuts to a close-up of geologist and author Ben Gad, an older man with a white beard and a green cap, holding a rock and speaking directly to the camera. The 'NAKED SCIENCE' logo appears in the lower third, identifying him. Ben Gad, whose clear and informative voice is now heard, gestures with the rock, emphasizing his explanation as he begins to describe the "pushing and shoving business" of geological forces, set against the scenic mountain backdrop.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Ben Gadd, identified by a lower-third graphic as 'Ben Gadd, Rockies Geologist and Author' under the 'NAKED SCIENCE' logo, standing in a mountainous outdoor environment with snow-capped peaks and evergreen trees under a partly cloudy sky. With a full white beard, wearing a green cap and light beige jacket, he gestures with his hands, which the camera zooms in on, to explain geological concepts. He uses his hands as an analogy, describing the "bony part" as a continental plate and the "skin" as sedimentary layers, explaining in a clear, explanatory voice how the "skin" wrinkles up when pushed along the "bony layer," forming mountains. He concludes his explanation by humorously remarking, "The older I get, the better this works for some reason." The scene then transitions to a 3D animated sequence illustrating tectonic plate boundaries. Two continental plates are shown moving horizontally over a glowing orange-yellow molten core, with yellow arrows indicating their divergence. A body of water between them recedes, forming a rift that gradually fills with sediment, visually representing continental rifting and seafloor spreading. A male narrator with a deep, authoritative voice, accompanied by dramatic, building background music and sound effects, explains that "Over millions of years, the plate collision forced up the Northern Rockies." He continues, stating that when the collision's force diminished, the first wave of mountain building ended, but "80 million years after the first block of land hit the coast, a second one slammed into its back," hinting at further geological events.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dramatic, orchestral score as a 3D animated cross-section of Earth's crust illustrates tectonic plate boundaries. A narrator explains how the process "bulldozed everything eastward and upward," with "layer after layer of rock was shoved higher and higher," forming "hills and then into mountains." This visual depicts two continental plates diverging over a molten core, causing a body of water to recede and a rift to form, which gradually fills with sediment. The animation transitions to a real-world aerial view of a lush, green mountainous region with lakes and forests, followed by a satellite view of Earth highlighting the western coast of North America in green, as the narrator describes how "gentle folds of rock evolved into the mighty Rockies." The scene returns to the 3D animation, showing the plates continuing to diverge and sediment accumulating. The narrator continues, stating that "Today these slabs of land, called terrains, make up British Columbia." Breathtaking real-world footage then showcases snow-covered mountain peaks under a clear blue sky, with the camera gliding through narrow valleys and over glaciers, emphasizing the rugged terrain. The narrator explains that "As they continued to rise, the Rockies were continually sculpted by the momentous force of the elements," adding that "even as they were pushed up, erosion was starting to tear them down." The final scenes show a dramatic mountain range at sunset, with golden light illuminating rocky faces above a dense forest, transitioning to a darker, more shadowed view as clouds drift across the peaks, underscoring the dynamic and majestic nature of mountain landscapes, concluding with the narrator identifying them as "The Canadian Rockies."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with sweeping panoramic shots of the rugged mountain range, identified as The Rockies, showcasing steep, rocky peaks with patches of snow under a clear blue sky. A narrator describes these as "Jagged peaks, steep cliffs, and razor-sharp ridges carved from the sedimentary rock of an ancient seabed," accompanied by dramatic background music. The camera pans across the landscape, revealing dense evergreen forests and dramatic rock faces illuminated by golden sunlight. The narrator explains that "Despite being caught in a mighty plate collision and thrust up into towering peaks, the layers of rock in these mountains still lie almost horizontally." This visual explanation transitions into a 3D animated cross-section of Earth's crust, illustrating tectonic plate boundaries with two continental plates moving horizontally over a molten core, yellow arrows indicating direction, and a body of water receding as the plates diverge, forming a rift. The narrator states, "This provides a clue about how they rose up." The animation shows the rift gradually filling with sediment, visually explaining the geological formation of mountain ranges. The narrator elaborates, "As the blocks of land drove into the coast of North America, slabs of sedimentary rock were pressurized and folded so much that they snapped," punctuated by a sharp snapping sound effect. The animation then transitions back to real-world footage of the same mountainous terrain, now under a partly cloudy sky with pink-tinged clouds, emphasizing the layered rock formations and the dynamic interplay of light and shadow across the peaks. The narrator concludes, "Broken sheets of rock, up to a mile thick, shunted forwards and slid over each other again and again, in a process scientists call thrust faulting," as the dramatic music swells.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Ben Gadd, a Rockies Geologist and Author, in a sunlit, rocky mountainous environment, wearing a green cap and beige jacket. The 'NAKED SCIENCE' logo appears as he speaks directly to the camera in a clear, explanatory voice, gesturing to describe how rock can be folded to a breaking point, leading to what he calls a 'thrust fault,' a classic feature of the Canadian Rockies. A consistent background music track begins and continues throughout. The scene then transitions to breathtaking panoramic shots of a serene alpine lake surrounded by towering, snow-capped mountains, with mist drifting over peaks and vibrant yellow wildflowers in the foreground. A distinct narrator's voice explains that while sedimentary rock usually forms with younger layers on top of older ones, in the Rockies, thrust faulting has pushed older layers up and over younger ones, creating jumbled formations. The video cuts back to Ben Gadd, now seated on a rocky outcrop, holding his book, 'The Book of the Canadian Rockies'. He places the book on a rock and flips through its pages, using it as a visual aid to further illustrate how a thrust fault causes one part of the rock to break and slide a long way, until it's eventually stopped.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Ben Gadd, a geologist and author, seated on a rocky outcrop in a rugged mountain environment. Wearing a green cap with a mountain logo, a light beige jacket over a brown sweater, and khaki pants, he gestures toward 'The Book of the Canadian Rockies' resting beside him, which features a blue cover with a mountain image and white and yellow text. Gadd explains in a clear, instructional tone, "So now we have layers that were down here, pushed up over these layers. Old rock pushed up and over younger rock," as he flips through the book's pages, emphasizing the geological concepts. Upbeat, slightly dramatic instrumental music begins as the scene transitions to a 3D animated cross-section of tectonic plates. A narrator's voice takes over, explaining, "In this laboratory sandbox, the layers of colored sand represent layers of sedimentary rock. As the sand pushes eastward to the right, thrust faults form, one in front of the other." The animation shows a red block, representing a continental plate, moving horizontally over a molten core, with a white scale bar and numerical values decreasing (e.g., 139, 132, 124, down to 109), illustrating geological compression or convergence. This animation is overlaid on real footage of snow-capped peaks and layered rock formations, with dynamic lighting shifts from bright sunlight to cloud-dappled skies. The narrator continues, "Thrust faulting pushes older rock layers above younger, to create the distinctive saw-tooth ranges of the northern Rockies." The animation, intercut with wide shots of snow-covered peaks and close-ups of layered rock, visually reinforces the connection between the model and actual geology. The narrator details the timeline: "These mountains first emerged 140 million years ago. Thrust faulting first built the western ranges, and then crept eastward, forming a new range further inland. 120 million years ago, the main ranges appeared. And then even further east, the front ranges, 85 million years ago." The segment concludes with the animation showing the red block nearly fully advanced, with the number '109' displayed, as the background shifts to a sunlit mountain face with visible rock layers and snow patches, symbolizing the culmination of tectonic forces shaping the Rockies.

Karlstrom, an older man with light-colored hair, wearing a dark blue shirt, standing outdoors with mountains in the background, speaking directly to the camera. A clear, authoritative male narrator introduces him as 'Structural geologist Karl Karlstrom from the University of New Mexico,' explaining he 'demonstrates using blocks of ice' where 'red and green ice layers represent the mountains above ground, and the white ice their roots below ground.' The setting then shifts to a sunny outdoor environment with lush green foliage, where Karlstrom, now in a bright orange polo shirt with gray accents, conducts a hands-on experiment. Visually, he places two large, cylindrical blocks of white foam into a clear glass tank filled with water, demonstrating buoyancy and displacement, adjusting them and observing how they float, creating ripples and bubbles. On-screen text identifies him as 'Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom, University of New Mexico,' with the 'NAKED SCIENCE' logo displayed. Audibly, Karlstrom speaks clearly and articulately, explaining that 'mountains are not static things,' and the earth's surface is 'continually being reshaped by erosion, by rivers, by glaciers.' He demonstrates, stating, 'I'll just pull one of the blocks off,' explaining how 'the overall surface has gone down,' and 'this rock, which was deep in the earth a minute ago... has come to the surface. That's called rock uplift.' He continues, 'I guess I'll erode off the pink layer too,' describing a 'continent without a major mountain belt.' The background music continues throughout his explanation.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a breathtaking aerial view of Earth from space, showcasing North and South America against a starry background, accompanied by a cinematic, slightly ominous background score. As the camera transitions into a detailed topographical zoom into mountainous terrain, a narrator explains, "The Front Range in Colorado is a thousand miles from where the Pacific and North American plates actually meet." The scene then dives into a 3D animated cross-section of Earth's crust, illustrating tectonic plate movement with a continental plate sliding over a molten core, highlighted by arrows indicating direction and heat flow. The narrator continues, "Geologists have come up with an explanation. They believe that the subducting Pacific Ocean plate is responsible." A deep rumbling sound underscores the animation as the narrator details how "Ocean crust had been pushed deep into the mantle beneath North America for a hundred million years, when something unusual happened." The sequence alternates between the animated tectonic model and real footage of rugged, snow-dusted mountain peaks, emphasizing the geological forces at play. The music becomes more pronounced as the narrator explains, "70 million years ago, the Pacific Ocean plate started to subduct at a shallower angle. The narrator continues, describing how "Thrust up through layers of sedimentary rock, the broken granite became the Rocky Mountains of the South." The animation then shows vertical fractures and uplift. The video cuts to real footage of striking red rock formations, including balanced rocks and eroded cliffs, under a bright blue sky, as the narrator identifies them as "Red Rocks Park, Denver, Colorado," a "landscape forged by granite uplift." The final scene shows two individuals lying prone on a red rock surface, with a third man in a light green shirt kneeling beside them, appearing to conduct a hands-on demonstration or measurement. The narrator identifies this man as "Kirk Johnson," demonstrating "how these granite peaks punched up through the layers of red rock around them." Kirk Johnson then speaks, introducing his "assistants, Veronica, and Ian," as a vast arid landscape stretches into the distance.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a wide shot of a dry, sunlit hillside where three individuals are engaged in geological fieldwork, accompanied by a male narrator explaining that "dating the Rockies requires real detective work" and that "you get clues by digging up dirt," set against a backdrop of instrumental music. A man in a straw hat uses a pickaxe to break apart rocky soil, while two others, one in a striped shirt with a white visor and another in a light gray t-shirt and cap, kneel nearby, examining rocks and using hand tools. The camera zooms in on the man with the pickaxe, capturing the forceful impact as he chips away at the earth, then cuts to close-ups of hands carefully extracting and inspecting rock fragments. Dr. Kirk R. Johnson, a man with short brown hair and glasses, then speaks directly to the camera, explaining that "to understand mountains, you don't look at the mountains... you look at the debris from those mountains," likening the basin adjacent to the mountain range to a "garbage can" containing "sedimentary rock and fossils, volcanic ash beds, layers of lava" that allows them to "reconstruct the chronology of the uplifting mountain range." The scene returns to the fieldwork under bright sunlight, with the rocky, layered formations dominating the frame, as the narrator concludes that "this debris forms layers of sediment 10,000 feet thick beneath the city of Denver," while a sweeping panoramic shot reveals a distant city skyline, emphasizing the proximity of urban development to the natural terrain, all underscored by the instrumental music.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with an atmospheric background music as a sweeping aerial view showcases a rugged mountain range with snow-capped peaks and deep valleys, partially shrouded in clouds, establishing a grand geological context. A male narrator explains that "in their mission to date these massive peaks, Johnson and his colleagues search for the tiniest clues, like fossilized leaves and pollen." The scene transitions to a sunlit, arid hillside where a Field Geologist in a Straw Hat, wearing a gray shirt and khaki pants, actively uses a pickaxe to break apart rocky soil, demonstrating field excavation. He then holds up a rock fragment, revealing its layered texture and embedded fossils, emphasizing geological discovery. The setting shifts to a futuristic, dimly lit tunnel with blue and purple lighting, leading into a museum or lab environment. Inside, Dr. Doug Nichols, an older man with white hair and a light blue shirt, identified by a lower-third graphic from the Denver Museum of Nature & Science, speaks directly to the camera. The narrator introduces him, stating that "these microscopic grains of pollen provide a valuable lead." Dr. Nichols, in a calm, explanatory voice, elaborates, "Pollen can be used to date the uplift of mountains because plants that were living at the time the mountains were coming up were shedding their pollen, which was becoming incorporated into sediment, which was being eroded from those same mountains." He continues, "And I'm looking for the particular species of pollen that can tell me the age of the sample." The background includes a microscope and a computer screen displaying magnified fossil-like structures. The video then shows close-ups of Dr. Nichols examining a slide under the microscope, with the computer screen displaying a blue field of microscopic fossils, including a prominent circular specimen with radial patterns. His hands are shown handling a red-framed slide holder, placing a slide under the microscope, and adjusting the lens. The narrator adds that "each sample of sediment can contain thousands of fossilized pollen particles." The final frames focus on the computer screen, where a yellow box labeled 'SPOT' highlights a specific fossil, indicating detailed scientific analysis, as the narrator begins to state, "Nichols examines a single..." The 'NAKED SCIENCE' logo appears in the lower third during Dr. Nichols’ interview, reinforcing the program’s branding.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a computer screen displaying a magnified, blue-tinted microscopic image of fossilized structures, with a yellow box labeled 'SPOT' highlighting a circular specimen with radial patterns. A calm, authoritative male narrator explains that "Every plant genus has distinctive shaped pollen," and by identifying plant species and their age, "Nichols can start to date the exact uplift of Colorado's Rockies." The scene transitions to Dr. Doug Nichols, an older man with white hair and a white beard, wearing a light blue shirt, seated in a laboratory. He is shown using a microscope, adjusting its objective lens labeled '40x/0.95', and interacting with the computer screen displaying the same fossilized images. Speaking directly to the camera with a clear, articulate voice, Dr. Nichols states, "We pretty well understand that the uplift began 65 to 70 million years ago because we find distinctive kinds of pollen produced by plants that only lived 65 to 70 million years ago." As he speaks, sweeping panoramic views of The Rockies are shown, showcasing rugged mountain ranges, dense forests, and valleys under a partly cloudy sky, with sunlight casting dynamic shadows, emphasizing their vastness and geological scale. A deep, resonant hum begins to build tension in the background. The narrator then adds that "Sometime during this 5 million year period, Colorado's Rockies began to rise." The hum intensifies as the video transitions to a dramatic animated sequence: a mountain peak is shown from Earth's perspective, then the view zooms out into space, revealing Earth rotating against a starry backdrop. The animation continues with a close-up of a rocky asteroid, overlaid with scientific equations, colliding with Earth, creating a fiery explosion and debris, symbolizing a catastrophic geological event. The narrator's voice declares, "But geologists can narrow this window even further, thanks to one of the most catastrophic events in history," which is underscored by a loud whooshing and a sustained, rumbling explosion sound accompanying the asteroid impact. The final frame is a blurred, abstract image of the explosion, as the narrator states, "65.5 million..." before the audio cuts off.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a dramatic animated sequence, accompanied by intense, low-frequency music and impact sound effects, depicting a catastrophic asteroid impact on Earth, first from a coastal perspective with a fiery explosion and mushroom cloud, then from space with the planet engulfed in flames and debris. Narration explains that "An asteroid smashed into the Earth," wiping out the dinosaurs and leaving "a distinctive line in the sediments by Colorado's Rocky Mountains." The scene transitions to a desolate, post-impact landscape with dead trees and a smoldering horizon, followed by a close-up of a large, lifeless dinosaur, emphasizing extinction. The narrator continues, stating "This pale yellow layer in the rock contains the remains of the huge asteroid." The narrative then shifts to a real-world, sun-drenched arid landscape, where aerial shots reveal a dry, grassy hillside with sparse vegetation, accompanied by the sound of wind. The camera zooms in to show two field geologists— a woman in a white visor and striped shirt, and a man in a cap and light gray shirt— actively excavating and examining rock fragments. The man holds up a broken rock piece, revealing a fossilized structure with a distinct circular pattern. The narration explains that because the debris is "sandwiched between layers of debris that eroded off the Rockies," it indicates the Rockies were "up and eroding at the time of impact, 65.5 million years ago." The scene cuts to Kirk Johnson, a man in a straw hat, carefully breaking apart rock with a pickaxe, the distinct sound of which is heard. The narrator identifies him, stating "Kirk Johnson specializes in dating fossilized leaves," using them "to date the Rockies' birth even more precisely." The final shot transitions to an underwater seabed, showing a vibrant, textured ocean floor with coral-like formations in turquoise water, as the narration begins to say "Geologists know that..." before the segment ends.

The scene transitions to a dimly lit laboratory where a Scientist uses a microscope and computer, which displays magnified fossil-like structures, including circular, pinkish-red specimens highlighted by a 'SPOT' box. The narrator continues, stating that "Johnson's fossil leaves show that just a million years later, there were plants here." The video then shows an arid hillside where a Field Geologist, a Female Field Assistant, and a Male Field Assistant are actively excavating rocks, with a close-up revealing the Male Field Assistant holding a fossilized rock specimen with a concentric structure. The background music becomes more intense and driving as the narrator explains, "Johnson combines his findings with Nickel's pollen evidence to come up with a million-year window during which the Colorado Rockies must have emerged." A panoramic view of The Rockies, with rugged peaks and dense forests, is shown, emphasizing their grandeur. Dr. Kirk R. Johnson then appears, speaking directly to the camera from the arid hillside, reiterating in his clear, explanatory voice that "69 million years ago, Colorado was covered by a shallow sea," and "68 million years ago, the Rocky Mountains were up." He elaborates that the exact timing of their emergence within that one-million-year period is still being investigated. The segment concludes with an aerial view of a snow-capped mountain range, including Half Dome, as the background music swells and then fades.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with sweeping aerial and panoramic views of rugged mountain landscapes, identified by the narrator as "the American Rockies," which he states "had arrived" 68 million years ago. The camera pans across layered rock formations, including striking red sandstone cliffs, as the narrator explains that the landscape "evolves once again" as they continue south through the Rockies. A dramatic shift occurs with a satellite-style topographical view of a volcanic region, followed by intense, fiery close-ups of molten lava erupting. This volcanic activity is underscored by a dramatic rumbling sound effect and the narrator's explanation that "volcanoes appeared among the peaks" 35 million years ago, triggered by "changes deep beneath America," marking "a new explosive era." The background music intensifies and then shifts to a grander, more adventurous tone as the footage returns to high-altitude mountain scenes with snow patches and expansive tree-covered hills. The narrator concludes by mentioning traveling "2,000 miles through the Rockies" and "forward through their 200 million" year history.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment commences with a sweeping 3D satellite topographical view of a vast, arid landscape, featuring rugged mountain ranges, deep valleys, and scattered volcanic cones rendered in earthy greens, browns, and ochres under a clear blue sky, while a narrator announces, "We reach Albuquerque, New Mexico." Dramatic orchestral music underscores the narrator's explanation that a "dramatic change deep beneath America triggered the most explosive phase in the evolution of the Rockies." This narrative is visually punctuated by an abrupt transition to intense, fiery close-ups of a volcanic eruption, with molten lava bursting violently from a dark crater, spewing glowing orange and yellow fragments into the air amidst thick smoke. The scene then shifts to a simplified 3D animated cross-section of Earth’s crust, depicting a dark brown tectonic plate moving horizontally over a glowing orange-red mantle, with visible fractures and upward buckling forming mountain ridges. The narrator details, "70 million years ago, the subducting Pacific Ocean plate punched up huge blocks of basement granite, forming the Rockies of New Mexico." As the animation progresses, the surface layer deforms into undulating peaks and valleys, illustrating mountain range formation through tectonic uplift, with the molten mantle continuously pushing upward. The narrator further explains, "35 million years later, the Pacific Ocean plate began to peel away from the underside of the North American continent," and "As the ocean plate sunk deeper, warm mantle came into contact with the underside of the North American plate, right underneath parts of the Southern Rocky Mountains." The final frames focus on the newly formed, smooth, beige mountainous terrain, emphasizing the geological transformation from subterranean forces to surface topography, all under a consistent light blue sky, with the dramatic orchestral score continuing throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with the powerful whoosh and roar of a hot air balloon's burner igniting, producing a bright, intense flame that fills the frame with orange and yellow light, illuminating the interior of the multi-patterned envelope. A male narrator explains, "Just as the burner on a hot air balloon makes the air less dense, causing the balloon to float upward through the cooler air around it," as the colorful balloon, featuring yellow, red, blue, and green panels, inflates and ascends. The camera shifts to an expansive aerial perspective, showing the balloon drifting over a landscape of roads, buildings, and a river, while the narrator continues, drawing an analogy: "So warm, buoyant mantle rose up through the cooler, denser mantle beneath the mountains." He then introduces Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom, stating, "Karl Karlstrom thinks that this caused the Rockies to grow even taller." The scene cuts to Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom, identified by an on-screen graphic as 'Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom, University of New Mexico' under the 'NAKED SCIENCE' logo, speaking from the basket with a clear, slightly academic voice. He elaborates, "Well, let's think about uplift of mountains in the context of this analogy of a hot air balloon. We're floating high because we're more buoyant than the air around us." As he speaks, the burner sound is heard again, and he continues, "And if we want to go up, actually get still higher, uh, we'll just turn on the burner and uh, fire it up and then the air becomes lighter within the balloon, causing a dynamic or an active uplift." The video alternates between close-ups of Prof. Karlstrom and another man in a gray shirt and cap looking out, and wide aerial shots of the vibrant balloon against a clear blue sky, floating serenely over a valley with distant mountains, all while the 'NAKED SCIENCE' branding remains visible.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a close-up of a man in a gray shirt and cap alongside Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom in a hot air balloon basket, observing the landscape below, as a narrator explains how geological forces are "actually pushing up the mountains." Dramatic, suspenseful music underscores the scene as it transitions to a 3D animated model of a desert-like terrain, illustrating geological uplift from a smooth, undulating surface to rugged, mountainous peaks and valleys. The narrator continues, stating that "it's not just the mountains that were affected," but that "the upwelling mantle also stretched the crust above." This is visually reinforced by a cross-sectional animation of layered rock strata, where horizontal lines are compressed and folded by opposing red arrows, simulating tectonic forces. The narrator describes this as a "sandbox simulation" demonstrating the stretching process, explaining that "as the continent pulled apart, the crust thinned and fractured," forming "an immense crevice, called the Rio Grande Rift," which "opened up in the heart of the Rockies." The animation continues to show crustal thinning as the narrator details how the crust is over 30 miles thick in parts of the southern Rockies but "just 17 miles thick" beneath the rift. The scene then shifts to real-world footage of a vast, arid canyon landscape with layered red rock formations, sparse vegetation, and distant mesas under a hazy sky, as the narrator declares, "This thin crust triggered something dramatic." The camera pans across the terrain, revealing deep canyons and eroded cliffs, while the narrator explains that "under immense pressure from rising heat in the mantle, it gave way." The segment culminates in a dramatic close-up of a volcanic eruption, with bright orange lava and billowing smoke, emphasizing the power of geological forces, as the narrator concludes that "red hot magma blasted out onto the New Mexico landscape," ending with a fiery, intense view of molten rock accompanied by powerful sound effects.

The camera then moves to a sweeping aerial view of rugged mountainous terrain with layered rock formations and deep valleys, while the narrator explains, "Scientists believe that hot magma has surged up from the mantle many times over the past 35 million years." This transitions into a dramatic time-lapse of a mist-covered mountain range under shifting clouds, as the narrator adds, "These surges led to renewed volcanism in the Rio Grande Rift." The footage cuts to a close-up of glowing lava beneath dark, cracked rock, with the narrator emphasizing, "But this is not ancient history." An expansive aerial shot then reveals a river of molten lava flowing across a dark landscape at dusk, as the narrator details, "The last eruption, just 1500 years ago, flooded New Mexico with a river of molten lava 44 miles long." The scene shifts to a sunlit, rocky mountain ridge with lush green forests below, revealing a panoramic view of distant valleys and hills, prompting the narrator to ask, "Could such recent eruptions mean that hot magma is ballooning beneath Earth's surface today?" The segment concludes with a woman in a floral shirt holding a transparent, spherical object against a bright sky, smiling as she presents it to the camera, while the narrator poses the final question, "Could the Rockies of New Mexico still be on the rise?" The suspenseful instrumental music continues throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a close-up of a hand carefully placing a transparent funnel into a shallow, muddy water pool nestled between weathered logs in an arid desert landscape, as a male narrator introduces "Laura Crossey, a geologist at the University of New Mexico," who is "on a mission to find out." Subtle background music accompanies the scene as two individuals, a man in a dark blue shirt and sunglasses and a woman in a floral shirt (Prof. Laura J. Crossey), are seen crouched, assembling a water filtration system with clear tubing and a small glass apparatus. The narrator explains that Crossey, working with Carl Carlstrom, is focusing on a fault where hot springs bubble up, providing clues from gases in the water. The camera pans across the reddish-brown desert terrain before focusing on Prof. Laura J. Crossey, identified by an on-screen graphic, who speaks directly to the camera in a clear, explanatory female voice, detailing how "this point along this fault is a place where these fluids are finding their way to the surface because of the extension, the tectonic activity. We're able to come here and have a window into that deep, deep world all the way to the mantle." The video returns to close-ups of the filtration setup, showing water being drawn through the funnel and tubing into a glass container, with distinct bubbling sounds accompanying the visual of bubbles forming as it passes through a filter. The narrator elaborates that beneath Earth's surface, gases under immense pressure dissolve in water and escape as bubbles when they reach the surface. The man and woman are then seen sitting on the logs, observing the system intently, with Prof. Crossey holding the glass container. The narrator concludes by mentioning that analyzing these gas bubbles allows Crossey to discover carbon dioxide and traces of helium 3, as the final shot shows the water pool with the filtration system in place, emphasizing the scientific effort in the harsh, arid environment.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a weathered, textured rock surface, showcasing natural erosion patterns in earthy tones, as a narrator explains that "helium 3" and "byproducts of activity in the mantle" indicate that "rock in the mantle is melting and on the move." The scene transitions to a wide shot of two individuals silhouetted atop a reddish-brown hill at sunset, with distant mesas, while the narrator continues, comparing the geological process to "a rising hot air balloon." This visual metaphor comes to life as the perspective shifts to the interior of a hot air balloon, where a powerful burner ignites with a distinct whooshing sound, filling the vibrant yellow, red, blue, and green envelope with hot air. From the basket, two passengers, including Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom in a red jacket, gaze out as the balloon ascends. Sweeping aerial views follow, revealing a sprawling valley with a river, urban areas, and distant mountains bathed in golden sunlight, then a lush green valley and rugged, rocky peaks, and finally snow-dusted mountain ridges and deep valleys. Throughout these grand vistas, accompanied by a calm background score, the narrator emphasizes that "here in New Mexico, the Rocky Mountains are far from dead," and "could still be growing, thrust higher by rising mantle." Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom, identified by an on-screen graphic, then speaks directly, stating in a clear, articulate voice, "I think portions of the Rocky Mountains are going up dynamically... and certainly in the million-year time frame we will have more interesting, higher and more rugged mountains, I would predict," as the camera alternates between close-ups of the passengers and the expansive panoramic views, capturing the serene beauty and scale of the terrain from above.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a sweeping aerial view of a snow-covered mountain range, showcasing jagged peaks and expansive glaciers under a clear blue sky, as an ominous, serious musical score begins. A narrator states that geologists, having investigated the past, now look to a "bleak" future, declaring, "The Rockies are doomed, and North America's destiny hangs in the balance." The camera glides over deep crevasses and rocky outcrops, emphasizing the rugged landscape. As the scene transitions to a misty, golden-hued mountain vista at dawn or dusk, with layers of peaks emerging from low-lying clouds, the narrator continues, reflecting on the "Rocky Mountains' 200 million year history," their "dramatic birth," and "complex evolution," before turning to their "uncertain future" tied to the North American continent. The light shifts, and the camera pans across a lush valley with dense evergreen forests and a meandering river, leading to distant snow-capped mountains. The footage then focuses on a massive, debris-covered glacier nestled in a U-shaped valley. The narrator identifies this as "the Columbia Icefield in Alberta, Canada," where geologists are already observing how the mountains are changing, predicting that "within a hundred years, it could look something like this." The camera moves closer to reveal the glacier’s cracked, slowly flowing surface, with ground-level shots showing meltwater streams carving through the ice and pooling in reflective puddles, as the sun glints off the water. The narrator concludes with a somber note, stating that "human activity is altering the course of geological history," underscored by the dramatic and mournful music.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with close-up shots of a rocky, debris-covered glacier surface, revealing crevasses and meltwater flowing through cracks, emphasizing the dynamic nature of the ice. A narrator's voice, accompanied by a serious background score, states that "increasing global temperatures are taking their toll on the mountains' glaciers," and "they're melting." The scene transitions to a wide panoramic view of the vast glacier in a mountain valley, with rugged peaks and clouds overhead, where a group of hikers can be seen walking across its surface, underscoring its immense scale. The narrator introduces "Brian Luckman from the University of Western Ontario," who "wants to know how fast" the glacier is melting, as a subtle helicopter or drone sound briefly passes. The camera then focuses on Prof. Brian Luckman, an older man with a white beard, wearing a wide-brimmed hat and a blue jacket, carrying a yellow backpack with a mounted device, walking across the rocky terrain. He is identified by an on-screen graphic as 'Prof. Brian Luckman, University of Western Ontario' under the 'NAKED SCIENCE' logo. The narrator explains that "during the winter, glaciers grow," and an "advancing glacier pushes a pile of eroded rubble ahead of itself, called a moraine." The video includes time-lapse footage showing the glacier’s retreat from 2001 to 2005, with years appearing on screen to mark the progression, illustrating the environmental change. The narrator continues, explaining that "when the glacier shrinks back in summer, it leaves behind this pile of debris in its wake," which is "a tell-tale sign that the glacier is melting away." Prof. Luckman, speaking with a clear, slightly academic British/Canadian accent, elaborates on this, stating, "Because the glacier over time is retreating continuously, then what you have left are a series of these annual moraine ridges, which mark the furthest points of advance during the previous winter." The background music becomes slightly more prominent before the narrator concludes, describing Luckman walking "between the moraines, using GPS to map the distance between" them, all set against the consistent backdrop of a high-altitude, arid, rocky landscape with a large glacier under a partly cloudy sky, focusing on both the natural environment and the scientific observation of glacial retreat.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide shot of a vast, debris-covered glacier in a mountainous valley, with a man in a blue jacket and wide-brimmed hat walking across the rocky foreground, as the year '2006' appears on screen. Ethereal, melancholic background music plays while a calm male narrator explains that "the greater the distance between them, the faster the glacier is melting." The scene transitions to a close-up of the same man, identified as Prof. Brian Luckman, an older man with a white beard, wearing a patched wide-brimmed hat and a yellow backpack. He speaks directly to the camera with a concerned, experienced expression and a distinct accent, stating, "Glaciers are one of the most effective ways to demonstrate to people that the climate is changing. You've only got to come here twice or look at some of the older photographs to realize something radically is changing." The footage then shifts to a series of aerial and panoramic views of glaciers and mountain ranges, showing the same glacier from different angles and time periods, including a 2007 timestamp and time-lapse footage from 2001-2005, highlighting its retreat. The narrator returns, detailing how "these two photographs of the three and a half mile long Athabasca Glacier show how far it has retreated over the past 30 years," and how "these photos of the Grinnell Glacier in Montana show that America's glaciers are disappearing as well." Dramatic shots of snow-covered peaks, deep valleys, and a turquoise glacial lake emphasize the landscape's scale and beauty, while the music swells. The sequence concludes with sweeping aerial views of forested valleys and snow-capped mountains under a clear blue sky, showcasing the broader ecosystem as the narrator somberly predicts that "one day, the glaciers that have shaped these steep-sided peaks and U-shaped valleys will disappear," though "water erosion will continue to sculpt these mountains."

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with sweeping aerial shots of the vast, sunlit mountainous landscape of the Rockies, showcasing winding rivers, dense forests, rugged, snow-capped peaks, deep valleys, alpine lakes, and dramatic rock formations, all while a narrator describes forces "beating them down and softening their jagged edges." The scene transitions to Ben Gadd, a Rockies geologist and author, wearing a green cap and light beige jacket, standing before layered rock formations. He explains in a calm, clear voice that "the future of the Rockies is that they're eroding at the rate of about 600ths of a millimeter per year," a rate determined by measuring river water sediment. The 'NAKED SCIENCE' logo appears. The footage then cuts back to more sweeping aerial views of snow-dusted peaks, glaciers, and turquoise lakes, accompanied by a building, dramatic orchestral score. A narrator's voice returns, stating, "The relentless force of erosion has sealed the fate of the Rocky Mountains since they first began to rise," and later, "One day, they will erode away to nothing." Prof. Stephen T. Johnston from the University of Victoria, wearing a red jacket and glasses, appears speaking in front of a forested backdrop. The video briefly shows canoes gliding across a calm lake with mountains, followed by a dramatic waterfall cascading down a rocky cliff. Prof. Johnston, with a calm, explanatory tone, then describes how "The Rockies that we see today, the beautiful mountains, why tourists come here, why it's just uh one of the most dramatic places on the earth. Uh they're eroding away," noting that the draining rivers are "full of silt and sand." The segment concludes with Prof. Johnston speaking again, against a blurred, fast-moving natural background, as the music continues to play.

Crossey, identified by an on-screen graphic, is seen holding a handheld device and speaking to the camera, before crouching beside a wooden box with a solar panel, adjusting equipment. segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a yellow handheld GPS device and a red wooden box with a solar panel, indicating a field research setup in a dry, arid landscape. A man in a straw hat and light shirt is seen working with the equipment, while a woman in a white shirt and khaki pants, later identified as Prof. Mousumi Roy, sits nearby using a laptop connected to the setup. A narrator explains this is a "five-year project" whose aim is "to monitor the widening of the Rio Grande Rift," as a documentary-style background score begins. The camera pulls back to reveal a wide, expansive desert scene with red soil, scattered shrubs, and distant mountains under a bright blue sky with fluffy white clouds. The narrator continues, detailing how "the team is setting up GPS stations along the length of the rift, like this one in remote New Mexico, three hours from Albuquerque." The focus shifts to a weathered metal sensor mounted on a tripod, showing signs of exposure, likely part of a remote monitoring station. The sound of digging or drilling is heard as the narrator explains that "the GPS receivers are buried in the bedrock to a depth of around five feet," moving when "the earth moves." The scene then transitions to a wide shot of the landscape where the man in the straw hat stands alone, followed by the woman joining him. As they stand together, digital overlays appear above them, displaying GPS coordinates, suggesting a scientific measurement or geolocation experiment. The 'NAKED SCIENCE' logo appears in the lower third, and a graphic introduces 'Prof. Mousumi Roy, University of New Mexico.' Prof. Roy then steps forward, holding a small black device, and begins to speak, explaining that with "two GPS stations," they gain "information about relative motion along a line that connects the two stations." The narrator adds that "a network of GPS stations allows the team to build up a much more detailed map of the rift's motions," before Prof. Roy continues, stating that "by looking at the difference between the coordinates between the GPS stations through time, we get a good idea of the relative motion between..."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide shot of a dry, arid landscape under a bright blue sky with scattered clouds, where a woman in a white shirt and khaki pants, identified as Prof. Mousumi Roy, walks toward the camera while holding a small black GPS device. In the background, three men in straw hats and light-colored field attire stand spaced apart, each marked with a digital GPS coordinate overlay displaying precise latitude and longitude, indicating a geolocation experiment. A female narrator states, "Each of these stations," followed by a male narrator explaining that "The team has set up 24 GPS stations along the Rio Grande Rift." The scene transitions to a close-up of two researchers, both wearing straw hats, working beside a red wooden box equipped with a solar panel. One man adjusts a yellow GPS device connected to the box, while Prof. Roy uses a laptop on the ground, suggesting data collection or monitoring. The male narrator continues, noting, "It's early days, but the team expects to find that the rift is widening between 0.5 and 2 millimeters every year." After a whoosh sound, he adds, "It's a very slow process, but little by little, the continent is being unzipped." The camera then pans across sweeping desert vistas with red rock formations and eroded hills, emphasizing the remote, rugged terrain. The video cuts to an older man in a dark blue shirt, speaking directly to the camera with the desert landscape behind him, his face partially shadowed. He is heard explaining in an academic tone that "The Rio Grande Rift is a rift system that's causing a continent to split," and if this "process continues for tens of millions of years, you could eventually rift the North American continent apart." This is followed by Prof. Laura J. Crossey, identified by an on-screen graphic, a woman with long dark hair wearing a floral shirt, also speaking animatedly to the camera with a similar desert backdrop. She is heard stating, "There's a lot of indications that there's some action happening. Maybe some more is in the future for the Rocky Mountains and another few million years and there'll be greater tales to tell of active tectonism further to the north where this Rio Grande Rift is trying to work its way and crack the continent." The lighting remains bright and natural throughout, with the sun casting sharp shadows, indicating midday, all set to a subtle, atmospheric background music.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute begins with a brief, seemingly unrelated female voiceover about an unsuccessful attempt, as a stunning rotating view of Earth from space, showcasing North and South America, transitions into a topographical mountainous view. Upbeat, cinematic orchestral music then swells as the scene shifts to breathtaking aerial perspectives of snow-covered mountain peaks under a clear blue sky, with the sun casting sharp shadows and lens flares. A male narrator introduces "The story of the Rocky Mountains," explaining they "began with the birth of the North American continent" and could potentially "end with its death." The camera glides over rugged, snow-dusted ridges, revealing a vast valley below with a meandering river, lush green forests, and golden meadows, framed by towering granite cliffs. The narrator speculates about a distant future where "the Rio Grande Rift may split a continent," potentially dividing "North America" into "East and West America," but emphasizes this is "hundreds of millions of years into the uncertain future." Sweeping aerial shots of the Sierra Nevada range, highlighting iconic formations like Half Dome with patches of snow and low-hanging clouds, accompany the narrator's declaration that "Today, the iconic Rockies are in their prime." The sequence culminates in a majestic view of a massive, snow-capped mountain range, possibly the Himalayas, with glaciers and jagged peaks under a vibrant blue sky, as the narrator concludes that these "dramatic and resilient" mountains, which "divide a continent," will "unite us all in admiration" and "remain the backbone of North America for millions of years."

Johnson: A man with short brown hair and glasses, wearing a light gray collared shirt, standing outdoors in a rocky, arid landscape with layered red rock formations and sparse vegetation, speaking directly to the camera. He explains in a clear, explanatory voice that 69 million years ago Colorado was a shallow sea, and 68 million years ago the Rocky Mountains had emerged, noting the precise timing within that million-year period is still under investigation.

entity_library.3D Animated Prehistoric Marine Life: Colorful, computer-generated reconstructions of ancient sea creatures, including purple spiky organisms and tube-like structures, are animated moving across a sandy seabed, visually representing the 'tens of thousands of sea creatures' that, as the narrator describes, were trapped and fossilized in the accumulating sediments of the ancient shallow sea.

entity_library.Tectonic Plate Animation Overlay: A 3D animated cross-section of Earth's crust, initially showing a smooth surface developing into rugged mountains due to uplift, then transitioning to layered rock strata compressed and folded by red arrows, simulating tectonic forces. This animation illustrates the upwelling mantle stretching the crust, causing the continent to pull apart, thinning and fracturing the crust to form an immense crevice, the Rio Grande Rift, with the narrator detailing crust thickness variations.
entity_library.Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom: An older man with light-colored hair, wearing a red jacket and sunglasses, standing in a hot air balloon basket, smiling and speaking. entity_library.Two prone individuals: One individual in a striped white-and-black shirt and green cargo pants (Veronica), and the other in a beige t-shirt and blue jeans (Ian), both lying face-down on a red rock surface. They are used as a live demonstration, symbolizing 1.7 billion-year-old metamorphic rocks. Ian's 'granitic back' is later exposed as the tarp is lifted, representing the uplifted core of the Rocky Mountains, while Veronica remains covered, symbolizing deeply buried sediments.
entity_library.Olive-green tarp: A large, heavy-duty olive-green tarp dramatically thrown over the two prone individuals, symbolizing layers of sedimentary rock covering the underlying metamorphic rock. It is later adjusted and partially lifted by the man in the green shirt to reveal 'Ian,' demonstrating the erosion and exposure of deeper rock layers during mountain uplift.
